question: How does the NuScale passive safety approach compare to active safety systems in conventional PWRs?

nuscale_ch01_0150.title: DSRS 3.11, Rev. 0: Environmental Qualification of Mechanical and Electrical EquipmentII.15 Application of RG 1.100 for Seismic Qualification of Electrical and Active Mechanical Equipment and Functional Qualification of Active Mechanical Equipment for Nuclear Power Plants
nuscale_ch01_0150.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory guidance for the environmental and seismic qualification of mechanical and electrical equipment in nuclear power plants. Specifically, it notes that the application of RG 1.100 for seismic and functional qualification of electrical and active mechanical equipment only partially conforms, with further details referenced in Table 1.9-2.

nuscale_ch01_0020.title: Safety Considerations
nuscale_ch01_0020.summary: The NuScale design enhances safety by maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits combustible gases. Its passive safety systems and integral design eliminate large-break loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) scenarios and reduce reliance on external power, resulting in a lower core damage frequency compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0022.title: Engineered Safety Feature Materials
nuscale_ch01_0022.summary: The NuScale reactor’s containment vessel (CNV) is kept under vacuum during normal operation, which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits oxygen for safety. Its passive safety features, including the elimination of external coolant loops and reliance on passive cooling and emergency systems, result in improved safety, lower core damage frequency, and a smaller radioactive source term compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0021.title: Engineered Safety Features and Emergency Systems
nuscale_ch01_0021.summary: The NuScale reactor uses a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV) to reduce corrosion, improve leak detection, and limit oxygen for safety, while also eliminating the need for insulation and reducing debris. Its passive safety systems, integral design, and small fuel inventory enhance safety by removing the need for external power and minimizing the risk of large-break loss-of-coolant accidents, resulting in a lower core damage frequency compared to traditional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0289.title: SRP BTP 8-9, Rev. 0: Open Phase Conditions in Electric Power SystemB.2 Criteria for evaluating open phase conditions for active plant designs
nuscale_ch01_0289.summary: The section states that the criteria for evaluating open phase conditions for active plant designs (SRP BTP 8-9, Rev. 0, B.2) are not applicable to passive plant designs like NuScale. Therefore, these regulatory requirements do not need to be addressed for the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0829.title: SRP 17.5, Rev 1: Quality Assurance Program Description - Design Certification, Early Site Permit and New COL applicantsII.D Procurement Document Control
nuscale_ch01_0829.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with SRP 17.5, Rev 1 requirements for quality assurance in design certification, early site permits, and new COL applications, specifically regarding procurement document control and related criteria. It notes full conformance in most areas, but identifies that certain site-specific and operational responsibilities, such as document control, identification and control of materials, and inspection, are delegated to the COL applicant referencing the certified design. Some criteria are marked as not applicable, as they fall outside the scope of the design certification applicant’s responsibilities.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the major design features and parameters of the NuScale Power Plant with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It provides a side-by-side summary of key characteristics such as electrical output, core design, reactor coolant system, and safety components, highlighting the differences and similarities between NuScale’s modular design and conventional PWRs. The comparison is intended for reference and uses nominal values only.

nuscale_ch01_0192.title: BTP 6-5, Rev 3: Currently the Responsibility of Reactor Systems Piping From the RWST (or BWST) and Containment Sump(s) to the Safety Injection PumpsAll Various
nuscale_ch01_0192.summary: This section explains that the guidance in BTP 6-5, Rev 3 applies only to light-water reactor ECCS designs that use safety injection pumps and refueling water storage tanks, which the NuScale ECCS design does not utilize. Therefore, the guidance is not applicable to NuScale, as its system does not rely on these components.

nuscale_ch01_0063.title: Emergency Core Cooling System Valve Design Certification Application Demonstration Test
nuscale_ch01_0063.summary: This section describes the demonstration test program for the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves in the NuScale reactor design. The program aimed to verify the functional performance of the ECCS valves, including their operation under reactor conditions and in borated coolant, as well as the effectiveness of the inadvertent actuation block (IAB) feature. Testing was conducted at a specialized facility using a test article representative of the reactor recirculation valve (RRV) to ensure the valves meet safety and design requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0024.title: Emergency Core Cooling System
nuscale_ch01_0024.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design provides passive removal of decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) using three reactor vent valves and two recirculation valves, all of which are closed during normal operation. During an emergency, the system vents steam into containment, condenses it, and recirculates water to cool the core, relying entirely on passive heat transfer to the reactor pool.

nuscale_ch01_0275.title: DSRS 8.4, Rev 0: Station BlackoutII.1 Compliance with 10 CFR 50.63 and the guidelines of RG 1.155
nuscale_ch01_0275.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s compliance with regulatory requirements for station blackout (SBO) and related DC power systems. The NuScale design partially conforms to 10 CFR 50.63 and RG 1.155, as all safety-related functions can be maintained without AC power for 72 hours, and the use of alternate AC power sources is not applicable. Independence of SBO-related power supplies is addressed, but some requirements are the responsibility of the COL applicant, and certain guidelines are not fully applicable to the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0278.title: SRP Appendix 8-A, Rev1: General Agenda, Station Site VisitsAll Various
nuscale_ch01_0278.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory criteria related to DC power systems and station blackout requirements. It notes that NuScale partially conforms to several regulatory guides, with some requirements not applicable due to the design’s passive safety features and the responsibilities assigned to the COL applicant. Additionally, SRP Appendix 8-A is identified as not applicable, as it pertains to NRC staff site visits during licensing reviews rather than design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0209.title: DSRS 7.2.1 Rev. 0: Quality II.2 Conformance to RG 1.152
nuscale_ch01_0209.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory guidance for I&C systems important to safety, specifically noting that Quality II.2 requirements align with Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.152. The document states that NuScale’s design meets the criteria of RG 1.152, with further details referenced in Table 1.9-2.

nuscale_ch01_0321.title: SRP 9.2.2, Rev 4: Reactor Auxiliary Cooling Water SystemII.1 Protection Against Natural Phenomena
nuscale_ch01_0321.summary: The NuScale Reactor Component Cooling Water (RCCW) system partially conforms to SRP 9.2.2, Rev 4, Section II.1, as it is not safety-related and thus follows less stringent seismic design requirements (Seismic Category II) per regulatory guidance. The system does not fully meet criteria intended for safety-related systems, but ensures that failures will not impact the operability of safety-related structures, systems, and components.

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information. It references tables and figures that list and explain the symbols used in these diagrams, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0202.title: DSRS 7.1.5, Rev 0: Diversity and Defense in DepthII.1 Methods for performing D3 analyses of reactor protection systems
nuscale_ch01_0202.summary: This section summarizes how the NuScale reactor protection system’s instrumentation and control (I&C) design conforms to various regulatory requirements for diversity and defense-in-depth (D3) analyses. It details compliance with specific regulatory guides, standards, and criteria—including NUREG/CR-6303 and SECY-93-087—demonstrating that the D3 assessment and quality assurance measures meet the applicable safety and reliability guidelines.

edges.description: Section A outlines regulatory requirements for the environmental and seismic qualification of active mechanical and electrical equipment, while Section B describes NuScale's passive safety features that reduce reliance on such active systems, thus demonstrating how NuScale's approach addresses or potentially obviates some of the qualification requirements discussed in Section A.
